notes.anchor: knocked off one by one through formal announcements of bad omens
notes.note: The obnuntiatio: a magistrate or tribune could halt or postpone elections by declaring he had observed unfavourable omens. Here good men welcome the delays because the consuls are tainted by a bribery scandal.

notes.anchor: the suspicion of bargains promised by the candidates
notes.note: A notorious scandal of 54 BC: the consular candidates had secretly agreed to pay the sitting consuls in exchange for help securing their election, a pact (coitio) that came to light and discredited the consuls.

notes.anchor: Gabinius has been put on trial for canvassing
notes.note: A. Gabinius, consul 58 BC and proconsul of Syria, faced multiple prosecutions on his return in 54 BC; the ambitus (electoral bribery) charge here was one of several. The named subscriptores are his prosecutor P. Sulla's relatives acting as supporting counsel.

The charge is maiestas: the offence of diminishing the majesty of the Roman state, tried before a standing court. Gabinius faced it for restoring Ptolemy Auletes to Egypt without senatorial authorisation.

A rhetor was a teacher of declamation, the staple exercise of Roman higher education. Cicero contrasts Paeonius's declamatory drill with his own more philosophical, thesis-based method of training young Quintus.
